title: Bias, variance and regularization

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Bias, variance and regularization; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Generalization is performance on relevant unseen data; underfitting reflects insufficient useful fit, overfitting reflects adaptation to sample noise, and regularization or data changes trade bias and variance.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Training and cross-validation curves show both errors high for an overly simple model, motivating features or capacity rather than stronger regularization. Your goal is to interpret learning or validation results and justify a regularization or data response. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Data and experimental workflow; Conditional probability and Bayes rule; Linear, neighbor and tree models; Train, validation, test and leakage. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Generalization is performance on relevant unseen data; underfitting reflects insufficient useful fit, overfitting reflects adaptation to sample noise, and regularization or data changes trade bias and variance. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Train-validation gaps are diagnostic but not unique causes, more data may preserve bias, regularization parameters must avoid test tuning, distribution shift differs from variance, and one split gives noisy evidence. The learner must reject this overclaim: A large train-validation gap always means the model needs more data, and the test set can be checked repeatedly to choose regularization.

includes: Diagnose underfitting and overfitting from learning evidence and control complexity without test-set tuning.
Interpret learning or validation results and justify a regularization or data response.
excludes: Train-validation gaps are diagnostic but not unique causes, more data may preserve bias, regularization parameters must avoid test tuning, distribution shift differs from variance, and one split gives noisy evidence.

outcomes: Interpret learning or validation results and justify a regularization or data response.

examples.title: Bias, variance and regularization: bounded direct case
examples.situation: Training and cross-validation curves show both errors high for an overly simple model, motivating features or capacity rather than stronger regularization.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: Training and cross-validation curves show both errors high for an overly simple model, motivating features or capacity rather than stronger regularization.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Diagnose underfit, variance, leakage, and shift with learning/validation evidence; tune without the test set and report variability across valid splits.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Diagnose underfit, variance, leakage, and shift with learning/validation evidence; tune without the test set and report variability across valid splits.

examples.title: Bias, variance and regularization: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: Near-zero train error with unstable validation performance leads to simpler capacity and repeated grouped folds while the test stays untouched.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: Near-zero train error with unstable validation performance leads to simpler capacity and repeated grouped folds while the test stays untouched.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Train-validation gaps are diagnostic but not unique causes, more data may preserve bias, regularization parameters must avoid test tuning, distribution shift differs from variance, and one split gives noisy evidence.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A large train-validation gap always means the model needs more data, and the test set can be checked repeatedly to choose regularization.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Interpret learning or validation results and justify a regularization or data response.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: A large train-validation gap always means the model needs more data, and the test set can be checked repeatedly to choose regularization.

analogy.example: a measured prediction workshop that compares a simple baseline with several fitted rules on held-out outcomes can introduce Bias, variance and regularization only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: regression, classification, model assumptions, and generalization
limitations: prediction evidence does not establish causation, fairness, or unchanged performance after deployment
